Course Title: Cybersecurity Risks in Public Sector AI Systems
Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: Protecting critical services from disruption and misuse.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with everyday digital tools and a general awareness of how public sector organisations use technology is all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the most common cybersecurity risks associated with AI systems used in public sector organisations and explain how these vulnerabilities can disrupt critical government and educational services. You will also apply practical strategies to recognise, report, and help mitigate threats, ensuring that AI-powered public services remain secure, trustworthy, and resilient against misuse.

lessons.lesson title: Cybersecurity Risks in Public Sector AI Systems
lessons.Description: Public sector organisations are increasingly adopting artificial intelligence to deliver essential services, from healthcare and benefits processing to law enforcement and infrastructure management. This lesson introduces the key cybersecurity risks that arise when AI systems are deployed in these high risk environments, including data breaches, adversarial attacks, and the misuse of automated decision-making. You will explore real-world examples of how vulnerabilities in AI systems can disrupt critical services and undermine public trust. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of the threat landscape and the practical steps organisations can take to protect their AI-powered systems and the citizens who depend on them.
lessons.content: Protecting critical services from disruption and misuse.

slides.subtitle: Understanding the stakes of government AI security
slides.slidecontent:
• Healthcare systems processing patient data
• Benefits processing for vulnerable citizens
• Emergency response and public safety tools
• Education platforms serving millions
• Critical infrastructure management
• Real consequences when systems fail

slides.subtitle: High-value targets with unique vulnerabilities
slides.slidecontent:
• Massive databases of citizen information
• Essential services millions depend on
• Often limited cybersecurity budgets
• Trusted at national scale
• Attractive to state-sponsored actors
• Disruption creates maximum impact

slides.subtitle: How cybercriminals exploit AI vulnerabilities
slides.slidecontent:
• Data poisoning during training phases
• Model theft through query manipulation
• Adversarial inputs causing wrong decisions
• Prompt injection in language models
• Hidden backdoors in datasets
• Exploitation of AI-specific weaknesses

slides.subtitle: When corrupted training data undermines AI decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• False data injected into training sets
• AI learns from compromised foundations
• Difficult to detect once embedded
• Example: fraud systems approving bad claims
• Can occur during updates and retraining
• Small corruption creates large impact over time

slides.subtitle: Protecting against risks from within your organisation
slides.slidecontent:
• Staff misuse of legitimate system access
• Contractors and vendors pose additional risks
• Privilege creep increases exposure over time
• Monitor unusual queries and data exports
• Restrict model retraining permissions
• Regular access reviews prevent accumulation

slides.subtitle: Protecting AI from input manipulation attempts
slides.slidecontent:
• Subtly altered inputs cause system errors
• Common in image recognition and fraud detection
• Input validation catches suspicious data
• Stress-testing reveals system weaknesses
• Clean training data governance essential
• Human oversight at critical decision points

slides.subtitle: Comprehensive protection strategies for AI systems
slides.slidecontent:
• AI-specific security audits and assessments
• Staff training on recognising abnormal behaviour
• Layered access controls for system interaction
• Updated incident response for AI failures
• Continuous output monitoring for drift
• Partnership with national cyber agencies

slides.subtitle: Essential practices for ongoing AI protection
slides.slidecontent:
• Verify all AI data sources and quality
• Monitor systems for unusual patterns
• Implement least-privilege access controls
• Train teams on AI-specific threat recognition
• Establish rapid incident reporting procedures
• Maintain current policies and threat awareness

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on cybersecurity risks in public sector AI systems. To understand why security matters so much, let's first look at where AI is actually being used in government today. Healthcare systems are processing sensitive patient records and making diagnostic recommendations. Benefits processing systems determine who receives critical social support. Emergency response tools help coordinate public safety efforts. Education platforms serve millions of students and families. And AI increasingly manages critical infrastructure that keeps our communities running. When these systems work properly, they deliver essential services efficiently and fairly. But when they fail due to security breaches or attacks, the consequences aren't just technical problems - they directly impact people's health, safety, and wellbeing.

So why do cybercriminals and hostile actors specifically target AI systems in the public sector? The answer lies in what these systems represent: incredibly high-value targets with unique vulnerabilities. They hold massive databases containing the personal information of millions of citizens - health records, financial details, and sensitive government data. They power essential services that entire populations depend on daily, meaning any disruption creates widespread impact. Many public sector organisations face budget constraints that limit their cybersecurity investments, potentially creating security gaps. These systems operate at national scale with high levels of public trust, making them especially appealing to sophisticated attackers, including state-sponsored groups looking to cause maximum disruption or steal valuable intelligence.

Understanding how attackers actually exploit AI systems helps us better defend against them. Unlike traditional cybersecurity threats, AI systems face unique attack vectors. Data poisoning involves corrupting the datasets that AI learns from, causing it to make wrong decisions from the start. Model theft occurs when attackers send thousands of carefully crafted queries to reverse-engineer how a system works. Adversarial inputs use subtle manipulations invisible to humans that completely fool AI into incorrect outputs. In language-based systems, prompt injection attacks hide malicious instructions within seemingly normal text. Backdoors can be planted in training data long before a system goes live. Each of these methods exploits the specific ways AI systems process information and make decisions.

Let's examine data poisoning in detail, as it represents one of the most insidious threats to AI systems. In these attacks, cybercriminals secretly inject false or misleading information into the datasets used to train AI models. Because the AI treats this corrupted data as legitimate, it begins making decisions based on fundamentally flawed information. What makes data poisoning particularly dangerous is how subtle and hard to detect it can be - there's no obvious alarm or system failure. Consider a real-world example: if attackers poisoned the training data for a fraud detection system, it might learn to approve fraudulent benefit claims instead of flagging them. This type of attack can happen during initial training or when systems update themselves with new data, and even small amounts of corrupted information can significantly skew results over time.

While external threats get most attention, some of the most serious risks come from inside your own organisation. Insider threats involve staff, contractors, or third-party vendors who have legitimate access to AI systems but misuse that access, either deliberately or accidentally. A common problem is privilege creep, where individuals gradually accumulate system access they no longer actually need for their role. To manage these risks, organisations should monitor for unusual patterns like excessive queries or bulk data exports from AI systems. Access to retrain or directly modify AI models should be strictly limited to essential personnel. Regular access reviews help identify and remove unnecessary permissions before they create security vulnerabilities. The key is recognising that insider risk is manageable, but only with active monitoring and governance.

Adversarial attacks represent a sophisticated threat where attackers feed AI systems carefully crafted inputs designed to cause errors or unsafe outputs. These attacks are particularly common in image recognition systems, fraud detection tools, and automated decision-making platforms used across public services. The manipulations are often so subtle that humans wouldn't notice anything wrong, but they can completely fool an AI system. Defending against these attacks requires multiple layers of protection working together. Input validation and anomaly detection help catch suspicious data before it reaches the model. Regular stress-testing against known attack patterns helps identify system weaknesses early. Maintaining clean, well-governed training data reduces the risk of manipulation taking hold. Most importantly, keeping human oversight at critical decision points provides a vital safety net when automated systems might be compromised.

Building comprehensive security resilience requires a systematic approach across multiple layers of AI system protection. This starts with regular security audits specifically tailored to AI components and data pipelines, not just standard IT assessments. Staff training becomes crucial so team members can recognise when AI systems are behaving unusually or producing unexpected outputs. Layered access controls should limit who can query, modify, or retrain AI systems, as this access represents significant power over system behaviour. Incident response plans need updating to cover AI-specific failure scenarios, which can look very different from traditional cyber incidents. Continuous monitoring of model outputs helps detect signs of manipulation or drift from expected performance. Finally, collaboration with national cyber agencies keeps organisations informed about emerging AI threats as they develop.

Let's conclude with a practical action framework for ongoing AI security. Always verify your AI data sources and quality, because compromised or poor-quality data leads directly to compromised decisions. Monitor your systems continuously for unusual patterns or anomalies that might indicate an attack or system drift. Implement least-privilege access controls, limiting system interaction to only those who genuinely need it for their roles. Train your teams specifically on AI threat recognition, as these risks look different from traditional cybersecurity issues. Establish clear, rapid incident reporting procedures, because delays in response only make problems worse. Finally, maintain current policies and threat awareness, regularly updating your approach as the threat landscape evolves. These practices won't eliminate every risk, but they provide a solid foundation for protecting AI systems and the citizens who depend on them.

Who is this for: Government employees, public sector administrators, civil servants, local government staff, educational administrators, school district personnel, non-profit managers, public service coordinators, municipal workers, city planners, social services staff
Team or Dept this is for: Government Operations, Public Administration, IT, Information Security, Risk Management, Compliance, Legal, Education Administration, Human Services, Public Safety
